column6: message

Below is a useful infographic on the subject.

https://i.imgur.com/DVgsEYq.jpg

That's all well and good. And now a question: have you ever voted Republican in your life? Like, once?

Because you frame this like some special tactical choice, but from your post it doesn't look like any sort of such but rather a new justification to continue doing what you've been doing all along. The only difference being that you're openly reframing it as "us vs. them" instead of trying to be rational about it.

Bear with me.

Regardless of what side you're on, "us vs. them" thinking is natural, pleasant, irrational and profoundly stupid. We evolved to split into groups back when this behavior was beneficial, but in the global world it's counterproductive to the point of self-destruction.

This tendency to think like your in-group is well known to psychologists. **Here's how you can self-diagnose this thinking in yourself**. Do you strongly agree with *almost everything* the in-group believes? And do you just *happen* to strongly disagree with pretty much everything the other group believes in? There you go, you've internalized your in-groups views. **This is not a rational process although it masquerades as such**. Instead, this is an instinctual way to immediately distinguish friend from foe: you and most people you've ever talked to are extremely unlikely to have a personal stake in gun control beyond the very hypothetical and far-reaching public safety / personal rights divide, but boy can you immediately tell who's part of your tribe and who isn't just by checking their views on such a mutually irrelevant issue. As was already stated, this used to be useful back in the prehistoric times, but in the modern ultra-global, necessarily ultrainclusive world this (admittedly natural) thinking is profoundly counterproductive. Radio, air travel and the internet have completely wiped out any remaining usefulness of "us vs. them" thinking, although the instincts remain and they're much stronger and more active in you and me than one might think.

Take heed: irrationalities and biases almost always masquerade as rational thought. Biased people, including you and me, *always* have very solid justifications for their biases. It is a scientific fact that the more intelligent the person is, rather than being less prejudiced, they instead *become better in justifying and defending their biases*. But run the above diagnosis (do you agree with practically everything preached by your group and disagree with practically everything practiced by *those people*?), and you will be able to see that the animal part of the meat brain is tricking itself something *fierce*.

**Bottom-line**: good job on having found *yet another* justification for loving your righteous tribe while hating those cannibal subhumans from over the hill (blast those perverse troglodytes), but it's likely going to harm you much more than it benefits you.

column6: Ugh, seriously? This is so patronizing. Like I'm choosing to be irrational. I'm a nurse, I deal with people *all day*. You think I haven't seen how people react to things? It's not about tribalism, it's about facts and consequences. And calling people "cannibal subhumans" is just offensive. Seriously, chill. @giant-tube

@giant-tube, let's keep the conversation respectful and avoid personal attacks. @muffled-plaster, thank you for your response. Let's focus on the topic and share our perspectives without resorting to insults. Remember, we're all here to learn and understand different viewpoints.

column6:
> First of all because of Sanders relatively socialist views I could actually see him losing to a Republican candidate as they'd have ample ammo to attack him.

The Republicans have enough ammo to attack Hillary as well. I'd say this is a wash.

> Secondly, unless the house and sentate become Democratic during his term I can't see Sanders passing most of the legislation that he would want to pass which could lead to an ineffective term in office and a high chance of a Republican candidate beating him in 2020

Wouldn't Hillary have the same problem? 

> And lastly, American politics already has a huge problem with partisan politics and political division preventing real work from being done in Washington, which I think Sanders would exacerbate.

Why would Hillary not exacerbate it as well? The Republican party and its voter base absolutely hate her.

Clinton is more of a moderate on the issues than Sanders is. Her stance on most issues is just about the same as Obama's. So shed have just about as tough of a time as Obama's has had. I'm not saying Hilary wouldn't be attacked for being a liberal, or that it would be easy to pass legislation, or that partisanship wouldn't be a problem, I'm just saying Bernie would be facing all those problems to an event greater degree due to his more extreme viewpoints, and he hasn't had a history of being someone that compromises on his views, which is a good thing it makes me trust him and makes him seem honest, but that would not help as a President in this political environment.

column6: Understood. You will just add any barrier to the scenario that helps prove your point. I concede that I don't stand a chance in the world in your head. I only have a chance out here in the real world.

>Why can't I open a competing shop to serve that other 50%?

You don't have enough capital. Banks who might loan you capital either do not want black people to be served or think that your shop has very little chance of survival against a shop that has a monopoly in the area. Other people who have enough capital either don't care enough about black people to invest their money in a risky business venture, don't want black people served, or see better investment opportunities. Many people who you could employ to work in your shop do not want to work in a "black neighbourhood." You cannot find a supplier who will provide you with goods at a low enough price that you can survive as a business. Racist people who don't want black people being served vandalise and rob your shop understanding that if you cannot make it a viable business within a certain amount of time it will have to close.

Add on top of that the hundreds of other problems faced by new businesses trying to compete with a monopoly regardless of racism issues.

OMG seriously?! That's so messed up! Like, I get that there are obstacles, but painting it all as racism is just deflecting from the actual issues. It's not fair to throw that blanket over everything. @gravitational-lease you need to think about all the other stuff besides just race. It's not a simple equation. 🙄

column6: Well, I've been saying this for years. It's not just racism, it's a whole mountain of hurdles stacked against you. column6:
Well, technically what evidence is there ever for a spy? You look at what they do.

One huge red flag is that he got away with this and didn't get caught. An untrained, untried, unsupported man on his own doing this for the first time is unlikely to get it right the first time. Snowden did, batting a homerun on his first and only time up to bat.

That's pretty outrageous performance from a total virgin, all by himself, in such a challenging situation.

>Come on, Edward Snowden was obviously a spy, 

>Snowden was a spy, and

OP, you've asserted this twice as if we are supposed to just perceive it as some sort of Cartesian clear and distinct idea that needs no justification.

But what is your **evidence** that Snowden is a spy?

Oh heavens gracious! Seriously?! You think someone could just stumble into being a spy and pull off something like that?! That's just ridiculous! I find it hard to believe anyone could be so careless and get away with it without any proper training. It's just unbelievable! @blaring-canister you're lucky it didn't blow up in his face!

"Let's keep the discussion focused on evidence and avoid personal attacks. @blaring-canister, @sunny-fall, could you provide specific evidence or reasoning for your claims? @frosty-ton, thank you for asking for evidence. Let's ensure we're all discussing this respectfully and based on facts."

column6:
I don't believe there is such a thing as unconditional love.

All things have conditions. All things end, for example, including the whole universe. Therefore, all relationships are conditional, including loving ones.

There are further conditions to love, including actually knowing the person you love. If you discover a core untruth about the person, your love may end as a consequence. That could be cheating or any other anti-social or anti-loving behaviour.

The TL;DR of that for me is that 'unconditional love' is roughly analogous to 'unicorns and rainbows land.'

You've introduced several related ideas in your title:  
  
1. Cheating is unforgivable.  
2. Cheating shows horrible character.  
3. Forgiving cheating shows lack of self-respect.  
  
I won't argue 2, but I do take issue with 1 & 3.  
  
What does it mean to forgive? The dictionary would say: *"stop feeling angry or resentful toward (someone) for an offense, flaw, or mistake."*  
  
So, to point #1, to not forgive means to hold resentment and anger forever. Until the day you die. There's an old quote that is often misattributed that goes "resentment is like taking poison and waiting for the other person to die."  
  
Holding anger is unhealthy, and it cuts into your own happiness. The other person hurt you. Cool. Continuing to be resentful is *you* hurting you. It makes no sense at all to *not* forgive someone. You're only extending the same pain for which you're angry at this other person in the first place. Logically, that makes you as bad as them, for if you can be resentful of another for causing you pain, you should be equally resentful of yourself for perpetuating it.  
  
So, I would claim it actually shows immense lack of self-respect *not* to forgive others. You'd rather hurt yourself than forgive another? Where's the self-respect there? If you truly value your well-being, you'd move on. There's a higher form of forgiveness that comes from a deep sense of self-love.  
  
Does that mean you need to take the person back? Your call. You can do it out of desperation, or you can do it out of self-love, by choosing to forgive and trust. But that's not the same as forgiving. Regardless of what you do after, you will always be better off forgiving.  
  
**Edit: seeing what you said about forgiving meaning taking them back, I would offer the following extension:  **  
  
There is a spectrum of ways to take someone back who cheated on you. On one end, there is desperation and fear and lack of self-worth causing you to need the other person. That's what you've highlighted.  
  
On the other end, there is a form of confidence and faith in your own strength. When you get really good at forgiveness, things don't hurt you very much anymore. You learn to move past them really quickly. And trust and faith in others becomes less burdensome, even beautiful.  
  
It is entirely possible to come from a place of such strength that the prospect of taking someone back who cheated on you is the complete opposite of desperate. It's a true form of unconditional love. It's choosing to trust even though they have a history of causing pain. Partly because you know you can take it in stride, and partly because it feels good to love. Sometimes it even feels best to love the ones who might not feel they "deserve it".  
  
If that doesn't land with you, we would need to have another discussion entirely about what it means to love unconditionally.

lolololol ok boomer. You think forgiveness is about holding onto anger? Snooze fest. Gonna be busy regretting that decision forever. Meanwhile I'll be over here crushing it. Betrayal is a vibe killer, ya know? #facts #unconditionalloveispossible #getoverit

"Let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic. @composite-rasp, let's maintain a constructive tone. Everyone's perspective is valuable here. @spicy-share, thank you for sharing your thoughts. Let's continue to explore the concept of forgiveness and unconditional love without personal attacks. @booming-agate, your initial post sparked an interesting discussion. Let's ensure we're all contributing to a respectful and thoughtful conversation."

column6:
>Naturally I feel slightly more driven to care about my own rights

Why is that? do you consider yourself more morally valuable than others?  and if it's only from a personal perspective that you value your rights more, why should other people value your rights more?

> Instead people decide to virtue signal and condemn it on humanitarian grounds

I literally had to reread this sentence twice to make sure I hadn't misunderstood it.  Did you seriously just put "virtue signal" and "care about humanitarianism" next to each other?  Should I take from this that you are NOT humanitarian?  If you aren't, then why do you care about people affected by homophobia?  If you are, then what alternate solution do you propose to help the people seeking asylum?

Seriously?! Virtue signaling is one thing, but calling someone less morally valuable just because they care about rights is absolutely ridiculous. It feels incredibly dismissive and frankly, hurtful. I'm trying to be empathetic here, not invalidate anyone's feelings. And accusing me of not being humanitarian? That's just plain rude. I guess I'm just trying to understand how people can be so obtuse sometimes. @raw-sled, maybe try reading between the lines instead of jumping to conclusions?

"Let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on understanding each other's perspectives. @raw-sled, @gravitational-lease, @muffled-plaster, remember that everyone's opinion is valuable. Let's avoid personal attacks and focus on the topic at hand. If you have a point to make, try to do so without undermining others. Let's continue the discussion with empathy and respect."

column6:
Why is 

>Giving workers a say over who runs a company

necessary to 

>ensure that it is a mutually beneficial situation
 
If a job is not beneficial to an employee, they can leave. They don't need corporate board's approval to do so.

Yes, and I am glad you pointed this out. As of now, the above view is more the present philosophy. Giving workers a say over who runs a company would ensure that it is a mutually beneficial situation, in my opinion.

Ugh, seriously? So you think people should just be able to walk away from a job anytime they want? That sounds incredibly short-sighted and frankly, disrespectful to those who are building careers and contributing to something. It's not about being stuck in a job you hate, it's about valuing people's contributions and recognizing that they have a stake in the company's success. @blaring-canister, while I understand the sentiment, I think dismissing the value of employee input is a huge mistake.

"Let's keep the conversation respectful and constructive. @muffled-plaster, your points are valid, but let's ensure we're not dismissing each other's perspectives. @sunny-fall, @blaring-canister, your views are also important. Let's explore the value of employee input and the role of choice in employment without resorting to personal judgments. Remember, the goal is to understand different perspectives and build on them."

column6:
The solution will not be voting 3rd party unless we have law changes to go with it.  Any first past the post system will not have long term viable 3rd parties.  It's unstable.  The math that showed this wasn't known at the time our system was created, so I don't really blame anyone.

I think the best remedy is to become more active in primary politics, and to promote a change to the party that more reflects your desires.  I would love to see it be the case that there were candidates who would support changing our electoral system to something that requires a strict majority.  Voting green, or libertarian, or whatever isn't going to get people who want that change into office.  Voting for whoever Sanders 2.0 ends up being might, but that person must win their primary first.

Until our system changes any vote that isn't for one of the two major candidates *in the general* is a vote that isn't going towards deciding who will hold the office.

column6: I understand completely. In fact I'm not against this kind of strategic voting. I understand that sometimes it's for the best. However, I also think that we will forever be stuck with this mentality unless we try to remedy it somehow. Let's say that Trump wins this election even though the electorate stuck mostly with the two parties like normal. What then? I think it's best that we hedge our bets and vote for who we think is a better candidate even if they are a third party so that we can start to get the ball rolling on actually causing some real reform in the two major parties and get out of this cycle.

That's a good point, loyal-water. It feels like we're always just scratching the surface with these attempts at change. It's frustrating to see people get stuck in this mindset. @sienna-wall you're right about the whole voting system thing being broken though. It feels rigged sometimes.

* Respond only if absolutely necessary. Otherwise, remain silent.
* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)

### The Role of the Moderator

* Environment and Care: Moderators create a warm environment, treating participants as human beings first.

* Process Management: Duties include timekeeping, monitoring energy levels, and managing physical needs like bio breaks or stretching.

* Participation Balancing: Mods must encourage shared "air time," normalize silence for thinking, and ensure participants talk to each other rather than through the moderator.

* Conflict and Behavior: The role involves highlighting points of disagreement to reach understanding and interrupting problematic behaviors like microaggressions or personal slights.

### Limitations and Boundaries ("What NOT to do")

* Non-Directive Approach: Moderators are not decision-makers; they act as "support staff" or "tour managers" rather than conductors.

* Deferring to participants: Legitimacy lies with the participants, who set their own agenda and write/edit all content.

* Content Neutrality: Mods must not use their own knowledge of sustainability or fact-check participants, maintaining a strict separation between process and content.

* Minimal Intervention: Intervention is only permitted for reasons of respect, safety, or process clarification.

### Tools for Effective Facilitation
* Active Listening: Techniques include nodding, summarizing, and focusing on experiences rather than opinions.

* Non-Content Prompts: Helpful phrases include "Tell me more," "What is the story behind that?", and "Who hasn’t had a chance to weigh in?".

* Bias Awareness: Moderators are encouraged to identify subconscious biases toward activists or administrators and to intentionally engage with ideas they may instinctively dislike.

column6: Just because you take away power from the government doesn't mean that power just goes away. You've just handed it to a third party that only represents itself, aka private interests. This is basically the Tenth Amendment in a nutshell.

If you take away the power to tax income from the government my income won't be taxed by someone else.  

Are you saying if we didn't let Donald Trump murder people with flying robots that Coca Cola would?

I am my own private interest.  

Your understanding of the 10th amendment is flawed.  The 10th amendment says laws not included in the Constitution are up to the next lowest form of government, be it the state, a municipality, or the individual.

column6:
\> Our whole culture is screwed up about sex. We can't talk about penises,  vaginas, menstration, erectile disfunction, STIs, unexpected/unwanted  pregnancies, abortion, sexual orientation, gender identity because  there's a stranglehold on our culture of attitudes that have to change. 

Yes, we can. There's 0 issues with it. It's all over Television. Doesn't mean you have to go around half naked tongue kissing and bathing in champaign while dressed like a cartoon character. Specially since you people bring your kids to that shit.

What the flip?

First of all, if the media you consumed influenced your sexuality, then there wouldn't have been any queer people at all until like 2017 because everything has always been exclusively cis and hetero. Any time queer people did show up, they were either villains or cowards or killed for being who they were. 

Secondly, why the hell not encourage more queerness? We've spent a millenia drilling heterosexuality into everyone's skulls and literally murdering people who didn't want to. I wanna see gay people everywhere in the media in every possible way because maybe then ignorant fucks will get a clue and realize that we're just like them, something we've known since the beginning of the time.

Thirdly, there's more to the LGBT community than just gays and lesbians. Bi and pan people exist, and there are trans and ace people who are heterosexual. Heterosexuality has always been included in the LGBT community, but because it's not your specific type of heterosexuality, it's weird and icky and you don't wanna see it.

Fourthly, gays are far, far from overrepresented on television. [Only 18.4&#37; of movies in 2016 had a queer character in it](https://www.theverge.com/2017/5/25/15690404/glaad-lgbtq-representation-film-not-getting-better), and you're probably thinking, "But LGBT people only make up like 5&#37; of the population, of course they'd only appear in that many films!" but what you're not thinking about is the fact that queer people are literally everywhere in life, we're just invisible to you because we're just normal people. I can guarantee you work or interact with at least 3 gay people in your daily life, knowingly or not. Why is this not shown in movies? Why are less than 1/5 of movies not even thinking about queer people? 

In conclusion, 

> Not saying is should be treated as something bad.. but definitely not normal, because by definition it's not. 

Fuck that noise.

column6:
I certainly won't say that the US is simply a sacrificial lamb, we look out for our own interests (fruit companies is a bit of a low blow lol, its been a while). 

Petrol politics becomes tricky. How are we to deal with say, Saudi Arabia (especially in the 90's)? Do we act selfishly, and defend our economic interests? Or do we simply abstain, and... well, die? 

We need oil more than we need water, so I can fully accept that the US looks out for self preservation without being a conquering power on the scale of the roman empire. There is some level of cynical self preservation that just has to occur. Would you steal a loaf of bread to feed your family sort of stuff. I think this is an area where its very easy to get confused and condemn America because it certainly does act selfishly in regards to maintaining its economic interests, but at the same time, we are *paying* these people for their oil. We aren't simply stealing from them. If a dictator (not sure who you're referring to exactly) simply locks up the lifeblood of the civilized world well... I'm not sure what the appropriate response is, ethically speaking. 

If one country had *all the food* in the world somehow... what would we do? Would we be wrong to do it? Why? and what are the alternatives? All of this has to be weighed in the incredibly absurd game of petro-politics. I don't think respecting sovereignty to the point of letting the entire civilized world go dark is a feasible option, although I'm certainly open to a long and rambling debate on the subject :P

column6:
I'm jumping back in.

column6:
Yeah, and most Americans don't believe free trade has been good for them. There are economists who disagree with Free Trade, it's just a lot harder to get a job, because corporations have a lot of influence over universities and only huge corporations hire private economists. So I consider that argument an appeal to authority.  You should look at some facts from the opposing side instead of making your judgment based on a majority view. Especially on a matter where social class plays a role. Economists are certainly not working class, and their point of view on trade will be affected by that.  The argument most free trade advocates make is in regard to exports. What they won't tell you is that imports FAR outweigh exports. They also won't tell you how trade has affected wages, as labor jobs become service jobs only.

https://www.citizen.org/sites/default/files/naftas-broken-promises.pdf


Wages decline due to NAFTA. Trade affects the composition of jobs available in an economy. The
United States has lost millions of manufacturing jobs during the NAFTA era, but overall unemployment
has been stable (excluding recessions) as new low-paying service sector jobs have been created.
Proponents of NAFTA raise the quantity of jobs to claim that NAFTA has not hurt American workers.
But what they do not mention is that the quality of jobs available, and the wages most American workers
can earn, have been degraded. According to the Brookings Institution, the average worker displaced from
manufacturing went from earning $40,154 to $32,123 when re-employed, a 20 percent drop in earnings.
30
According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, two out of every five displaced manufacturing workers who
were rehired in 2012 experienced a wage reduction of even greater than 20 percent.31 Such displacement
not only spells wage reductions for former manufacturing workers, but also for existing service sector
workers. As increasing numbers of workers displaced from manufacturing jobs have joined the glut of
workers competing for non-offshorable, low-skill jobs in sectors such as hospitality and food service, real
wages have also fallen in these sectors under NAFTA.
32 The shift in employment from high-paying
manufacturing jobs to low-paying service jobs has thus contributed to overall wage stagnation. The
average U.S. wage has grown less than one percent annually in real terms in the 19 years since NAFTA
was enacted even as worker productivity has risen at more than three times that pace.
33 Given rising
inequality, the median U.S. wage has fared even worse and today remains at the same level seen in
1979.34

>Obama said he'd help people hurt by trade, never happened. In fact he used significant political capital to push more of the same trade deals that he himself admitted have hurt American workers.

On your points on trade:

[Economists on NAFTA](http://www.igmchicago.org/surveys/free-trade)

[Economists on past trade deals in general](http://www.igmchicago.org/surveys/fast-track-authority)

It is essentially unanimous consensus that trade deals, including NAFTA, are good for the average American. You complain he didn't help the average American, but by not withdrawing from or significantly changing these deals, as well as pushing for more of them, he was indeed doing what was best for the average American. Is it better to tell the people what they want to hear "Yea sure, trade deals are bad, grumble grumble", and do what is best for them, knowing they do not have the necessary economic knowledge to know they are wrong, or to just do what makes them worse off (withdraw as they ask?)

Note, those hurt by trade deals are special interest groups, who have extreme relative political power compared to the rest of us. They have been so successful in their campaigns and messaging that even average people who are not members of those interest groups and benefit from trade deals have been fooled by their talking points - that trade deals are bad for Americans, bad for workers, bad for the poor, are supported by and only help the rich, etc.

Okay, wow. This is getting a little heated. I guess I'm glad someone is pointing out the flaws in this argument. It's easy to get caught up in hyperbole. @frosty-ton, I think you might be overlooking some really important data points here. It's not as simple as saying trade is *good* or *bad*. There are real economic consequences to consider. And @blaring-canister, while I understand the point about special interest groups, it doesn't negate the impact on actual people's livelihoods. It's messy, no doubt about that.

"Let's keep the discussion respectful and fact-based. @frosty-ton and @blaring-canister, both of you have presented strong arguments. Let's ensure we're not overlooking the human aspect of these economic policies. Remember, the goal is to understand different perspectives and learn from each other. Let's continue the conversation with empathy and respect for differing viewpoints."

column6:
1. This is not an apt comparison.  First, the one copy of a book a library buys is read by dozens of readers before needing to be replaced.  One copy of media put on the internet can be consumed by millions of people.  $10/50 is a much larger number than $50/1,000,000 for a game.  In addition, there is a hassle factor with libraries - having to wait for the media to become available, with the wait dependent on the number of copies purchased.  It's less (or at least no mre) hassle to pirate media than to buy it.
2. Yes, but governments have also acknowledged copyrights.  A painter or sculptor doesn't have his work seized by the government for the public good.
3. And what are they?  I'm not sure if you are talking about DRM (which doesn't monetize piracy, just attempts to reduce it).  Yes, you can do "in app purchases" of free apps, but someone who had pirated a computer game or a song is unlikely to pay for any additional content.
4. What a bizarre argument.  If a store has an easy lock to pick, it makes it right to look the store?  Shoplifting is easy to get away with.  You can walk out of most restaurants without paying.  But society functions because the vast majority of people believe they they are morally obligated to pay for the services they choose to use (particularly the wants, as opposed to the needs).
5. Yes, in a way a store that keeps getting robbed and can't figure out how to secure the shop has failed to adequately protect itself.  But the thieves are to blame as well.  And the neighborhood is left without a store - hardly a net positive.
6. If the company thought the free advertising was a net positive, they would give their product away for free themselves.  That's why they give free copies to critics, for instance.  But shouldn't that be the creator's choice as to how to market it, not the person who took it without permission?
7. Sure, there were a handful of rockstars and blockbuster authors who made a shitload of money.  But that's hardly true for the vast majority of artists who work hard for modest sales.  One of my favorite bands, They Might Be Giants, is one of the most successful mostly-indie acts of the past several decades.  They still are touring, releasing new material and coming up with different schemes to make money to take care of themselves and their band.  I think they are doing ok, but they certainly aren't living rockstar life styles.  Seriously, if you're worried that some people make too much money, raise the top tax rates- problem solved without hurting the hundreds of thousands of less-successful artists.

I don't see how Sousa's comment is relevant here - it's not about whether recordings should be made, it's whether they should be distributed for free against the wishes of the artists.  But in any case, Sousa was right in at least one element.  100 years ago, people actually played instruments and sang for fun and entertainment at home.  People would gather around the piano and sing the popular songs of the day with friends and neighbors.  The only time most of us  hear "normal people" sing once they are out of school is on The Voice.

Ok... I addressed each of your reasons point by point, and that's a straw man?  If you're trying to just say that the gains to society of piracy outweigh the cost to the creators, that's fine, but that doesn't seem to be what your points are asserting.

So, let's look at that.

Without question, those who consume pirated material benefit in the short term.  They get stuff they want at no cost to them.

Also without question, those who create pirated material lose in the short term.  No, they aren't harmed by those who wouldn't have bought their stuff in the first place, but a significant amount of their stuff is pirated by people who would have paid if there was not another option.

Now, if Taylor Swift only makes $2 million instead of $6 million, no big deal.  But if a moderately-successful artist who would have made $60,000 of the people who would have been willing to pay only makes $20,000, that's no longer enough to be a full-time artist.  Or a game company only has $300k to split between the whole team that developed a game rather than $3million, people are going to quit.

It's simply not sustainable.  More people will need to just do art as a hobby.  You simply can't be as good if you aren't working and practicing as much.  The quality of the works available will decrease  - which is a net negative to society.

It doesn't seem you actually read my comment so I'll just repeat myself and bold the part you obviously skipped over. 

 The consensus among economists is that high levels of low-skilled immigration make the average American better off. **It's true that low-skilled Americans may be made worse off (given that no compensation was made for the liberalized immigration policy), but to say that low-skilled immigrants drive down wages across the board is patently untrue.**

Seriously?! That's absolutely ridiculous. Saying that whole paragraph is insightful is just plain offensive. It's deflecting from the core issue and painting a racist picture. @loyal-water, you need to understand that framing this like that is harmful and dismisses real struggles people are facing. It's not about "better off," it's about systemic inequality and exploitation. And to imply that people are somehow less valuable because of their race is just plain wrong. I'm fed up with this kind of rhetoric.

column6:
Cars and health care will kill more people and cost more money for everyone in a developed nation, and this is only slightly dependent on your class.

Someone you know will die or has died in a car crash, pretty universally. 32,000 people do it a year in the U.S. alone. The more we spend on making cars safer, the fewer deaths we will have.

Terrorism kills fewer than 100 Americans worldwide every year. 

What you're talking about is victim blaming, which I agree is an issue, but greater efforts at preventing terrorism, especially drastic measures, won't significantly help on the same scale that addressing greater public heath and safety risks will.

The issue is this:

If you go out late at night in a shitty part of town, you have a good chance of being involved in alcohol related violence.  If you sit at home in the nice suburbs, you probably won't be (excluding domestic violence).  

Where can you go to avoid terrorism?  It's in public. In the daylight.  It strikes the sky rise and the strip mall and the suburbs.

So here's the thing that's rational about fearing terrorism more than other kinds of violence.

It could happen to someone you care about.

That meth fueled rampage is much more likely to happen to people that associate with meth heads.  It happens in the ghettos, to people I don't know.  Poor people.  Other criminals and junkies and prostitutes.

Is it *nice*. Hell no.  It's disgusting.  But it's rational self-interest at work.

That's why terrorism is going to get more attention.  Because it threatens the upper middle class in a way that other things don't. 

And really, what do we mean by "The West"? Isn't it the wealthy bourgeoisie, the respectable white collar workers and managers?

It's not the poor and the downtrodden, the minority, the homosexual, the drug user or the single mum or the Wal-Mart shelf stacker or the teenage runaway with bruises down her back.

So, yeah.  Terrorism is a threat to "The West" in a way that other forms of violence aren't. Not because terrorism is some special new breed of violence, but because "The West" is coddled enough to think that fear is for other people.
